questions.stem: A 65-year-old man with a 50-pack-year smoking history presents with sudden massive haemoptysis (approximately 400 mL over 2 hours). He has lost 5 kg in the past 3 months and has a persistent cough. On examination, he has clubbing, and auscultation of the chest reveals reduced breath sounds at the left apex. What is the single most likely diagnosis?
questions.explanation: The combination of heavy smoking, weight loss, persistent cough, and massive haemoptysis strongly suggests bronchogenic carcinoma. The reduced breath sounds at the left apex may indicate a mass or post-obstructive changes. Clubbing can occur in lung cancer, though less specific. Tuberculosis is more common in younger or immunocompromised, and aspergilloma typically complicates a pre-existing cavity.

questions.stem: A 32-year-old woman with a history of recurrent chest infections since childhood presents with massive haemoptysis (300 mL in 24 hours). She has had a chronic cough with purulent sputum for years. On examination, she has bilateral coarse crackles at the lung bases and digital clubbing. What is the single most likely diagnosis?
questions.explanation: The history of recurrent chest infections with chronic purulent sputum, crackles, and clubbing points to bronchiectasis as the underlying cause of massive haemoptysis. Cystic fibrosis typically presents earlier in life with other features such as pancreatic insufficiency. Tuberculosis and lung abscess usually have systemic symptoms; pulmonary AVM is less common and often has no chronic respiratory symptoms.

questions.stem: A 38-year-old man who is HIV-positive (CD4 count 80 cells/µL, not on antiretroviral therapy) presents with massive haemoptysis (200 mL in one episode). He has a 2-month history of night sweats, low-grade fever, and weight loss. Chest X-ray shows a left apical cavity. What is the single most likely diagnosis?
questions.explanation: The patient is severely immunocompromised with classic symptoms of TB (night sweats, fever, weight loss) and an apical cavity on CXR. Tuberculosis is the most likely cause of massive haemoptysis in this setting. PJP typically presents with bilateral interstitial infiltrates and cough, not cavity. Aspergilloma can occur in a pre-existing cavity but is less likely without a prior TB history.

questions.stem: A 22-year-old woman with no significant medical history presents with sudden massive haemoptysis (400 mL) over 30 minutes. She has had recurrent epistaxis since childhood. On examination, she has multiple telangiectasias on her lips and fingers, and chest auscultation is clear. What is the single most likely diagnosis?
questions.explanation: The combination of recurrent epistaxis, telangiectasias, and massive haemoptysis in a young woman is highly suggestive of hereditary haemorrhagic telangiectasia (Osler-Weber-Rendu) with pulmonary arteriovenous malformations. The clear chest and lack of other symptoms make bronchiectasis, TB, and mitral stenosis unlikely. Pulmonary embolism typically presents with pleuritic pain and dyspnoea.

questions.stem: A 55-year-old man with a history of treated pulmonary tuberculosis 20 years ago presents with massive haemoptysis (250 mL in 24 hours). He has had a chronic cough for years with occasional purulent sputum. CT chest shows a thin-walled cavity in the right upper lobe containing a rounded mass. What is the single most likely diagnosis?
questions.explanation: The history of prior TB and CT finding of a cavity with an intracavitary mass (fungus ball) is classic for aspergilloma. Recurrent TB would typically show consolidation or new cavities, not a discrete mass. Lung abscess usually has an air-fluid level. Carcinoma is less likely in the context of a pre-existing cavity and no significant smoking history mentioned.

questions.stem: A 55-year-old man presents with massive haemoptysis (300ml in 1 hour) and is haemodynamically stable. He has a 40-pack-year smoking history. Chest X-ray shows a mass in the right hilum. What is the most appropriate next investigation to confirm the cause?
questions.explanation: CT thorax with contrast is the next step after chest X-ray to characterize the mass and identify any associated bronchiectasis or vascular anomalies. Bronchoscopy is often performed later for tissue diagnosis, but CT is preferred initially for comprehensive anatomy.

questions.stem: A 30-year-old woman with known hereditary haemorrhagic telangiectasia presents with massive haemoptysis and oxygen desaturation. CT chest shows a large pulmonary arteriovenous malformation (AVM) in the right lower lobe. What is the best next investigation to plan treatment?
questions.explanation: Pulmonary angiography is the gold standard for defining the feeding artery and draining vein of a pulmonary AVM, essential before embolization. Contrast echocardiography is a screening test, not for treatment planning.

questions.stem: A 50-year-old woman with no known lung disease presents with sudden onset of pleuritic chest pain and haemoptysis of 200ml. Her D-dimer is elevated. Chest X-ray is normal. What is the most appropriate next investigation to confirm the diagnosis?
questions.explanation: CT pulmonary angiogram (CTPA) is the first-line investigation for suspected pulmonary embolism, which can present with haemoptysis. CT thorax without contrast would not assess pulmonary arteries adequately.

questions.stem: A 28-year-old pregnant woman (32 weeks gestation) presents with haemoptysis of 250ml. She has no known lung disease. Chest X-ray is unremarkable. What is the most appropriate investigation?
questions.explanation: Flexible bronchoscopy is safe in pregnancy and can localize the bleeding source, avoiding fetal radiation exposure. CT thorax would expose the fetus to ionizing radiation and is not first-line.

questions.stem: A 45-year-old woman with known severe bronchiectasis presents with a third episode of massive haemoptysis. She is stable. Previous CT scans show widespread bronchiectasis but no active bleeding. What is the most appropriate investigation to guide management?
questions.explanation: Flexible bronchoscopy can identify which bronchial segment is actively bleeding, crucial for targeted bronchial artery embolization. CT may not show active bleeding in the setting of known bronchiectasis.

questions.stem: A 45-year-old man presents to the emergency department with sudden onset of massive haemoptysis (approximately 500 mL in the last hour). He has a known history of left upper lobe bronchiectasis. He is haemodynamically stable. CT pulmonary angiography shows active bleeding from a tortuous bronchial artery. What is the single most appropriate first-line definitive therapy?
questions.explanation: Bronchial artery embolization is the first-line definitive therapy for massive haemoptysis from bronchiectasis, as it directly occludes the bleeding vessel. Surgery is reserved for failure of embolization or when embolization is not feasible.

questions.stem: A 70-year-old woman with known COPD and bronchiectasis presents with haemoptysis of 100 mL over 24 hours. She is haemodynamically stable. Sputum culture grows Pseudomonas aeruginosa. CT thorax shows cylindrical bronchiectasis but no active bleeding. What is the single most appropriate first-line therapy for this episode?
options: Surgical resection of the affected lobe
Nebulized colistin 2 million units twice daily
Intravenous ceftazidime 2 g three times daily
questions.explanation: In mild-to-moderate haemoptysis due to infective exacerbation of bronchiectasis, treatment of the underlying infection is the priority. Intravenous ceftazidime is appropriate for Pseudomonas coverage; embolization or surgery are reserved for massive or persistent bleeding.

questions.stem: A 60-year-old man with a history of treated pulmonary tuberculosis presents with massive haemoptysis (600 mL over 2 hours) and hypotension. CT shows a 1.5 cm pseudoaneurysm in a right upper lobe cavity (Rasmussen aneurysm). He is stabilized with fluids and remains tachycardic. What is the single most appropriate first-line definitive therapy?
options: Interventional radiology with a covered stent
questions.explanation: Bronchial artery embolization is the first-line treatment for a Rasmussen aneurysm causing massive haemoptysis, as it directly occludes the bleeding vessel. Surgery is reserved if embolization fails or not available.

questions.stem: A 55-year-old woman with known bronchiectasis underwent bronchial artery embolization for massive haemoptysis 2 days ago. She now has recurrent haemoptysis of 300 mL. Repeat angiography shows no identifiable bleeding source. What is the single most appropriate next definitive therapy?
options: Transfer to high-dependency unit for observation
questions.explanation: When bronchial artery embolization fails for massive haemoptysis, surgical resection of the affected lobe is the definitive therapy for rebleeding. Repeat embolization is less likely to succeed if no source is identified.

questions.stem: A 30-year-old woman with hereditary haemorrhagic telangiectasia (Osler-Weber-Rendu syndrome) presents with haemoptysis of 200 mL. CT shows a pulmonary arteriovenous malformation (AVM). She is haemodynamically stable. Which of the following therapies is CONTRAINDICATED in her management?
questions.explanation: Anticoagulation (e.g., warfarin) is contraindicated in hereditary haemorrhagic telangiectasia due to the high risk of severe bleeding from vascular malformations. Embolization of the AVM is the definitive treatment.

questions.stem: A 55-year-old smoker presents with a single episode of coughing up 150ml of bright red blood. He is comfortable, saturations 97% on air, BP 130/80, pulse 90. No prior respiratory history. What is the single most appropriate immediate next action?
options: Refer to respiratory team for outpatient review
questions.explanation: In massive haemoptysis (≥150ml), the immediate step is to localise the bleeding source. CT thorax with contrast is the initial investigation of choice as it can identify the cause (e.g., tumour, bronchiectasis) and guide intervention. Chest X-ray may miss the source, and bronchoscopy is typically performed after CT. Tranexamic acid is not first-line, and outpatient referral is inappropriate.

questions.stem: A 70-year-old with known lung cancer presents with coughing up 300ml of blood over 10 minutes. He is drowsy, saturations 88% on 15L oxygen, BP 85/50, pulse 120. What is the single most appropriate immediate next action?
options: Request urgent anaesthetic review for airway management
questions.explanation: This patient has massive haemoptysis with airway compromise and haemodynamic instability. Airway protection is the priority. Requesting immediate anaesthetic assistance to secure the airway (e.g., intubation) is the critical first step. IV fluids and cross-match are important but secondary.

questions.stem: A 60-year-old with massive haemoptysis due to bronchiectasis underwent bronchial artery embolisation (BAE) 8 hours ago. He now coughs up 200ml of fresh blood, saturations 94% on 4L oxygen, BP 100/60. What is the single most appropriate immediate next action?
questions.explanation: In rebleeding after recent BAE, repeat embolisation is the definitive intervention if the patient is relatively stable. Recurrent bleeding often indicates incomplete embolisation or recanalisation. CT may be considered but delays treatment. Intubation is not yet needed as saturations are reasonable.

questions.stem: A 75-year-old on warfarin for atrial fibrillation presents with haemoptysis of 100ml over 30 minutes. INR 4.2. No respiratory distress, saturations 96% on air, BP 130/70. What is the single most appropriate immediate next action?
questions.explanation: For life-threatening bleeding (massive haemoptysis) on warfarin, rapid reversal is indicated with prothrombin complex concentrate (PCC). Vitamin K works too slowly for acute bleeding. Stopping warfarin alone is insufficient. Imaging can be performed after stabilisation.

questions.stem: A 28-year-old primigravida at 28 weeks gestation presents with a single episode of haemoptysis (approximately 100ml). She is otherwise well, saturations 99% on air, BP 110/70. No prior respiratory history. What is the single most appropriate immediate next action?
questions.explanation: In pregnancy, chest X-ray with shielding is the first-line imaging for haemoptysis as it has minimal fetal radiation. CT and V/Q scans involve higher radiation or contrast risk. Bronchoscopy is invasive and not first-line. Antibiotics are not indicated without evidence of infection.

questions.stem: A 60-year-old man with known bronchiectasis presents with a 1-hour history of coughing up bright red blood. His blood pressure is 130/80 mmHg and heart rate 88 bpm. On examination, he appears calm but is using accessory muscles of respiration. Which single finding on examination should prompt immediate escalation?
questions.explanation: Stridor indicates upper airway obstruction from a blood clot, which can lead to asphyxiation. Cyanosis is a late sign of hypoxia but stridor is more immediately life-threatening as it signifies impending airway compromise.

questions.stem: A 35-year-old woman with no past medical history presents with a small amount of haemoptysis following a dental extraction. She is haemodynamically stable. Which examination finding would raise suspicion of a pulmonary arteriovenous malformation (AVM)?
questions.explanation: A bruit over the chest is a classic sign of pulmonary AVM due to turbulent flow through the malformation. Fever and pleural rub are more typical of infection or inflammation, and clubbing is not associated with AVM.

questions.stem: A 55-year-old man with known squamous cell carcinoma of the lung presents with sudden haemoptysis. Over 4 hours he has coughed up approximately 300 mL of blood. His oxygen saturation is 89% on room air, blood pressure 100/60 mmHg, and heart rate 112 bpm. Which clinical finding is most critical to identify?
questions.explanation: Altered mental status indicates inadequate cerebral perfusion or severe hypoxia, constituting a life-threatening emergency. The mild drop in haemoglobin, tachypnoea, hypercapnia, and focal crackles are concerning but less immediately critical than consciousness impairment.

questions.stem: A 25-year-old woman with systemic lupus erythematosus on mycophenolate mofetil presents with acute dyspnoea and haemoptysis. Chest X-ray shows bilateral alveolar infiltrates. Which diagnosis must be excluded as a matter of urgency?
questions.explanation: Diffuse alveolar haemorrhage is a life-threatening complication of SLE that can rapidly progress to respiratory failure. While pneumonia and embolism are also important, alveolar haemorrhage is the most urgent to identify because it requires immediate immunosuppression and ICU support.

questions.stem: A 70-year-old man with chronic kidney disease on warfarin for atrial fibrillation presents with new haemoptysis. His INR is 5.2. Which finding is most indicative of a massive bleed?
options: He coughed up 150 mL of blood in the past 24 hours
His oxygen saturation is 95% on room air
His haemoglobin dropped from 13.0 to 11.0 g/dL over 24 hours
questions.explanation: A drop in haemoglobin of 2 g/dL suggests significant blood loss, which may be greater than the visible haemoptysis volume. 150 mL in 24 hours is below the typical threshold for massive haemoptysis (>200 mL), and a stable oxygen saturation and normal platelets do not indicate massive bleeding.
